title: SqueezeNet
concept: SqueezeNet showed that a CNN can stay competitive while using far fewer parameters by squeezing channels with 1x1 convolutions before expanding them, proving that careful module design can do more with fewer weights.

exercises.prompt: Recap: in edge settings, why do smaller models matter so much?

options.text: They can reduce latency, memory use, and battery drain on limited hardware
They force every convolution to become 7x7

exercises.explain: Right — efficient deployment is the reason architectures like SqueezeNet were exciting.

cards.title: Travel-size CNN
cards.body: Imagine packing for a weekend trip and discovering you can bring almost everything you need with a much smaller bag. SqueezeNet tried that with CNNs: keep strong image-recognition ability while dramatically shrinking the parameter count.

cards.title: What is SqueezeNet's main idea?
cards.body: SqueezeNet is an efficient CNN family built around the fire module. A fire module first uses a squeeze step with 1x1 convolutions to reduce channel count, then an expand step that mixes 1x1 and 3x3 filters to build richer features back up. The goal is simple: spend fewer weights before the expensive spatial work.

cards.title: Narrow pipe, then shower head
cards.body: Think of water flowing through a narrow pipe before reaching a shower head. The narrow section limits how much water enters the expensive wide spray. In SqueezeNet, the squeeze layer narrows the channel flow before the expand layer spends cost on feature extraction.

cards.title: Why the squeeze saves parameters
cards.body: A 3x3 convolution gets expensive when it reads many input channels, because its weight count scales with 3×3×C_in×C_out. If a fire module first uses 1x1 filters to shrink C_in, then the later 3x3 filters read fewer channels. That means fewer weights to store and fewer multiplications to run.

cards.title: Small does not mean primitive
cards.body: SqueezeNet is not 'just a tiny weak CNN.' Its lesson is that a lot of parameter cost in older models was avoidable. With smart bottlenecks and more 1x1 work, you can preserve useful representational power while cutting model size dramatically.

cards.title: Inside a fire module
cards.body: The module deliberately compresses first, then spends compute more selectively.

exercises.prompt: Why does SqueezeNet place a squeeze step before part of its expensive 3x3 work?

options.text: To reduce the number of input channels before the heavier filters, lowering parameter cost
To make the image physically smaller on disk
To prevent the network from using any nonlinearity
To guarantee perfect accuracy on all datasets

exercises.explain: Correct — the squeeze layer is a bottleneck that makes later operations cheaper.

exercises.prompt: True or false: the fire module's expand step can include both 1x1 and 3x3 filters.
exercises.explain: True — that mix is part of how SqueezeNet stays expressive while staying compact.

exercises.sentence: SqueezeNet's fire module first reduces channels, then ___ them into richer features.

exercises.prompt: Which description best matches SqueezeNet's design philosophy?

options.text: Do more with fewer weights by bottlenecking channels before expensive convolutions
Use the largest possible filters everywhere regardless of cost
Avoid all reuse of 1x1 convolutions in CNNs

exercises.explain: Yes — SqueezeNet is a classic lesson in parameter efficiency.

exercises.prompt: A fire module receives many channels from an earlier layer. Which redesign most directly follows SqueezeNet's logic for reducing parameter count while keeping useful feature extraction?

options.text: Use 1x1 convolutions to squeeze channels first, then apply the expand step with 1x1 and 3x3 filters
Replace the whole block with a much wider 7x7 convolution
Duplicate all channels so later layers have even more to process
Remove all bottlenecks because bottlenecks can never help CNNs

exercises.explain: Exactly. SqueezeNet cuts cost by shrinking channel width before the more expensive part of the block. Next: MobileNet pushes efficiency even further by replacing many standard convolutions with depthwise-separable ones.
